minutes_briefs.title: Risk-based security screening starts for new BC cannabis licence applicants
minutes_briefs.summary: Starting May 21, 2026, the BC Liquor and Cannabis Regulation Branch (LCRB) will require all new cannabis licence applicants to go through a baseline security screening. This means you must complete a Cannabis Screening Form and submit a Police Information Check (PIC) or a Criminal Records and Judicial Matters Check. The new risk-based approach means low-risk applicants will face fewer delays, while applicants with certain risk indicators (like past criminal involvement or financial concerns) may be sent for a more detailed fit and proper assessment. This change also applies when you add or change associates (people who could influence the licence or the business) or when someone new is identified who could affect the licence.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Starting May 21, 2026, submit a completed Cannabis Screening Form and a Police Information Check with every new cannabis licence application.

key_dates.label: BC LCRB risk-based cannabis screening takes effect

minutes_briefs.source_body: This change applies to the following application: The Liquor and Cannabis Regulation Branch (LCRB) is introducing a risk based approach to cannabis security screening EffectiveMay 21, 2026, this change applies to all new cannabis applications submitted on or after this date. This change means that new cannabis applicants will now undergo baseline security screening, including completion of the Cannabis Screening Form and submitting aPolice Information Check(PIC) or a Criminal Records and Judicial Matters Check. This security screening process applies when applying for a new cannabis licence, when adding or changing associates who require screening, and when an individual is identified who may directly or indirectly influence the applicant or licensee, affect activities under the licence, or have connections to an associate of the applicant or licensee. Associates include individuals who may directly or indirectly influence the applicant or licensee, affect the activities proposed under the licence, or have connections to an associate of an applicant. This risk-based approach aligns cannabis screening with the liquor licensing model. It reduces unnecessary delays for low-risk applicants, applies investigative resources where they matter most, reduces processing timelines, and maintains public-safety and market integrity standards. Only applications that present identified risk indicators will be referred for a comprehensive fit and proper assessment. Risk indicators may include, but are not limited to: If further review is needed, the assessment may include additional security screening and a more detailed review of financial information. These changes are reflected in the updated: Bulletins are for general information and may not apply to all situations. Bulletins do not constitute legal advice nor are they a comprehensive statement of the legal obligations that arise under the Liquor Control and Licensing Act, Cannabis Control and Licensing Act, regulations, or any other applicable laws. When interpreting and applying the information contained in the Bulletins, you are encouraged to seek advice from your professional advisors as appropriate in the circumstances. The B.C.
